revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:小学校令]][[zh:小學校令]]
'''Shōgakkōrei''' (小学校令) refers to two [[Imperial ordinances]] that established the {{ill|primary education|en|primary education|qid=Q975085|lt=primary education|1=Primary education}} system in [[:en:Modern Japan|Modern Japan]]: * '''Shōgakkōrei''' (Imperial Ordinance No. 14 of April 10, Meiji 19) – Enacted in {{ill|1886|en|1886|qid=Q7822|lt=1886|1=1886}}, this was the first primary school ordinance, part of the so-called "[[Gakkōrei]]" series. * '''Shōgakkōrei''' (Imperial Ordinance No. 215 of October 7, Meiji 23) – Replaced the first ordinance in {{ill|1890|en|1890|qid=Q7832|lt=1890|1=1890}}. After a complete revision in {{ill|1900|en|1900|qid=Q2034|lt=1900|1=1900}} (Imperial Ordinance No. 344 of August 20, Meiji 33, referred to as the third Shōgakkōrei), it was revised and renamed the '''[[Kokumin Gakkōrei]]''' (Imperial Ordinance No. 148 of March 1, Shōwa 16) in {{ill|1941|en|1941|qid=Q5231|lt=1941|1=1941}}. == History == The first Shōgakkōrei was promulgated on [[April 10, 1886]], during the tenure of Minister of Education {{ill|Mori Arinori|en|Mori Arinori|qid=Q1194472|lt=Mori Arinori|1=Mori Arinori}}, replacing the [[Kyōikurei]] (Education Ordinance). In {{ill|1890|en|1890|qid=Q7832|lt=1890|1=1890}}, a new Shōgakkōrei (Imperial Ordinance No. 215) was enacted, abolishing the first ordinance<ref>Strictly speaking, the first ordinance was repealed in areas conflicting with the [[City Code]] and [[Town and Village Code]] through Imperial Ordinance No. 215, and fully abolished by the Elementary Education Regulations for Areas Without City or Town and Village Codes (Imperial Ordinance No. 40 of 1892).</ref>. This second ordinance was completely revised in {{ill|1900|en|1900|qid=Q2034|lt=1900|1=1900}} to form the third Shōgakkōrei. The ordinances are commonly referred to as the first, second, and third Shōgakkōrei. The third Shōgakkōrei remained in effect until {{ill|1941|en|1941|qid=Q5231|lt=1941|1=1941}}, when it was superseded by the Kokumin Gakkōrei. Technically, the final Shōgakkōrei existed until {{ill|1947|en|1947|qid=Q5263|lt=1947|1=1947}}, when the [[School Education Act]] abolished the Kokumin Gakkōrei. == First Shōgakkōrei == {{Japanese Law | Title=Shōgakkōrei | Alias={{Japanese Law/Alias|Q117378147|qq=}} | Number={{Japanese Law/Number|Q117378147|qq=}} | Status=Abolished | Type=[[Education Law]] | Enacted={{Japanese Law/Enacted Date|Q117378147|qq=}} | Promulgated={{Japanese Law/Promulgation Date|Q117378147|qq=}} | Effective={{Japanese Law/Effective Date|Q117378147|qq=}} | Content=[[Elementary schools]] regulation | Related=[[Kyōikurei]], [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]] | Link=[{{NDLDC|2944042/2|qq=}} Official Gazette, April 10, 1886] | Wikisource=Shōgakkōrei (Imperial Ordinance No. 14 of Meiji 19) }} The first Shōgakkōrei, promulgated on [[April 10, 1886]], consisted of 16 articles defining the establishment and operation of elementary schools. Su11lementary regulations on "[[Subjects and Levels of Primary Education]]" were issued on [[May 25, 1886]], outlining school organization, duration of studies, subjects, class sizes, teachers, and instructional days. === Content === * Elementary schools were divided into '''[[Jinjo Shōgakkō]]''' (four years of education) and '''[[Kōtō Shōgakkō]]''' (four additional years). * Compulsory education covered ages 6 to 14, with the four years of Jinjo Shōgakkō as the mandatory period. * Children unable to attend school due to illness, financial hardship, or other reasons could be granted deferred attendance by the prefectural governor or district head. * Class sizes were limited to 80 students for Jinjo Shōgakkō and 60 for Kōtō Shōgakkō. * Costs were primarily covered by tuition fees and donations, su11lemented by local municipal funds if necessary. * A simplified elementary curriculum ('''Shōgaku Kani-ka''') was permitted in financially constrained areas, replacing Jinjo Shōgakkō. These schools did not charge tuition fees. === Abolition === The ordinance was abolished with the enactment of the second Shōgakkōrei. == Second Shōgakkōrei == {{Japanese Law | Title=Shōgakkōrei | Alias={{Japanese Law/Alias|Q117378157|qq=}} | Number={{Japanese Law/Number|Q117378157|qq=}} | Status=Defunct | Type=[[Education Law]] | Enacted={{Japanese Law/Enacted Date|Q117378157|qq=}} | Promulgated={{Japanese Law/Promulgation Date|Q117378157|qq=}} | Effective={{Japanese Law/Effective Date|Q117378157|qq=}} | Content=[[Elementary schools]] regulation | Related=[[Kyōikurei]], [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]] | Link=[{{NDLDC|2945436/1|qq=}} Official Gazette, October 7, 1890] | Wikisource=Shōgakkōrei (Imperial Ordinance No. 215 of Meiji 23) }} The second Shōgakkōrei, promulgated on [[October 7, 1890]], replaced the first ordinance. It comprised 96 articles and included detailed provisions for the elementary education system, reflecting changes in local governance established by the [[City, Town, and Village Codes]] of {{ill|1889|en|1889|qid=Q7831|lt=1889|1=1889}}. === Changes === * Article 1 clarified the purpose of elementary schools: > "Elementary schools aim to nurture moral education, provide foundational national education, and impart essential knowledge and skills for daily life." * Simplified elementary schools were abolished. * Mandatory education was set at 3–4 years for Jinjo Shōgakkō. * High schools offered 2–4 years of education. * Introduction of vocational courses ('''Senshūka''') and su11lementary courses ('''Hoshūka'''), along with a11renticeships and vocational su11lementary schools. * Municipalities were required to establish Jinjo Shōgakkō for all school-age children. * Provisions allowed municipalities to use private elementary schools as substitutes. === Related Regulations === The ordinance required extensive su11lementary regulations, such as those governing private school accreditation, facilities, ceremonies, curricula, class organization, and teacher certification. === Partial Revisions === In {{ill|1899|en|1899|qid=Q7851|lt=1899|1=1899}}, provisions designating vocational and su11lementary schools as part of elementary education were repealed. == Third Shōgakkōrei == {{Japanese Law | Title=Shōgakkōrei | Alias={{Japanese Law/Alias|Q117378173|qq=}} | Number={{Japanese Law/Number|Q117378173|qq=}} | Status=Abolished | Type=[[Education Law]] | Enacted={{Japanese Law/Enacted Date|Q117378173|qq=}} | Promulgated={{Japanese Law/Promulgation Date|Q117378173|qq=}} | Effective={{Japanese Law/Effective Date|Q117378173|qq=}} | Content=[[Elementary schools]] regulation | Related=[[Kyōikurei]], [[Kokumin Gakkōrei]] | Link=[{{NDLDC|2948434/1|qq=}} Official Gazette, August 20, 1900] | Wikisource=Shōgakkōrei (Imperial Ordinance No. 344 of Meiji 33) }} The third Shōgakkōrei, enacted on [[August 20, 1900]], revised the second ordinance entirely. It comprised 73 articles and clarified educational objectives and structures. === Changes === * Unified mandatory Jinjo Shōgakkō education to four years. * Encouraged 2-year high school programs in preparation for extending mandatory education to six years. * Prohibited child labor during school hours and imposed penalties for non-compliance with attendance laws. * Declared public elementary education tuition-free in principle. === 1907 Revision === Mandatory education was extended to six years under the "Amendment to Shōgakkōrei" (Imperial Ordinance No. 52 of 1907). === Replacement by Kokumin Gakkōrei === In {{ill|1941|en|1941|qid=Q5231|lt=1941|1=1941}}, the ordinance was replaced and renamed under the Kokumin Gakkōrei. {{For2|Subsequent developments|Kokumin Gakkōrei|qq=}} == References == {{References|qq=}} == External Links == * [https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317552.htm Centenary History of Education] - {{ill|Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology|en|Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology|qid=Q1054379|lt=Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology|1=Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology}} (MEXT) {{Wikisource|Shōgakkōrei|qq=}} {{Wikisource|Subjects and Levels of Primary Education|qq=}} {{Gakkōrei|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|小学校令|version=102553444|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:小学校令]]
